steps.text: Confirm DKA: glucose >200 mg/dL + pH <7.3 + HCO₃ <15 mEq/L. Weigh child. Assess degree of dehydration (mild 5% / moderate 7% / severe 10%).
IV access × 2. Send labs: blood glucose, electrolytes (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻), BGA, urine ketones, HbA1c, blood culture.
Fluid resuscitation: 0.9% Normal Saline 10–20 mL/kg IV over 1–2 hours. Do NOT start insulin yet.
steps.trap: Start fluids BEFORE insulin. Do NOT give insulin in the first hour.

steps.text: Check serum potassium: if K⁺ <3.3 mEq/L → ADD potassium to fluids and HOLD insulin until K⁺ ≥3.3. If K⁺ >5.5 → hold potassium supplementation.
steps.trap: K⁺ <3.3 = HOLD INSULIN. This is the most dangerous DKA error — insulin + hypokalaemia = fatal arrhythmia.

steps.text: Start insulin: Regular insulin 0.05–0.1 U/kg/hr IV continuous infusion. NEVER give insulin as a bolus in paediatric DKA.
steps.trap: NEVER bolus insulin. Continuous infusion only. Bolus → rapid osmotic shift → cerebral oedema.

steps.text: Replace total fluid deficit over 48 hours (NOT 24 hours) using 0.45% NaCl + KCl 20–40 mEq/L. Rapid replacement causes cerebral oedema.
steps.trap: 48 hours — NOT 24 hours. Students frequently write 24-hour replacement. That is wrong.

steps.text: When blood glucose falls to 200–250 mg/dL: ADD dextrose (D5 or D10) to IV fluids. Continue insulin infusion — do NOT stop it.
steps.trap: Do NOT stop insulin when glucose normalises. Insulin must continue until ACIDOSIS resolves (pH >7.30, HCO₃ >18). Add dextrose to prevent hypoglycaemia.

steps.text: Monitor closely every 1–2 hours: glucose, electrolytes, BGA. Watch for headache, bradycardia, altered consciousness (cerebral oedema warning signs).
Cerebral oedema: Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV OR 3% NaCl 5–10 mL/kg IV immediately. Reduce fluid rate. Elevate head of bed 30°.
steps.trap: Both mannitol AND 3% saline are valid — know both. Must also reduce fluid rate simultaneously.

steps.text: Transition to subcutaneous insulin when: acidosis fully resolved (pH >7.30, HCO₃ >18), tolerating oral feeds, glucose stable. Overlap SC and IV insulin by 30 min before stopping infusion.
Confirm nephrotic syndrome: urine P/C ratio >2.0 mg/mg (or >50 mg/kg/day protein) + serum albumin <2.5 g/dL + oedema.
Check complement C3 and C4. If C3 is LOW → do NOT start steroids yet. Renal biopsy is needed first. Also biopsy if age >8 years or <1 year.
steps.trap: Low C3 + NS = biopsy BEFORE steroids. Starting steroids with low C3 (MPGN/other) is a major error.

steps.text: General measures: bed rest during oedema phase. Salt restriction (NOT protein restriction — maintain normal intake). Daily weight, BP, urine dipstick, I/O chart.
steps.trap: Protein restriction is WRONG in NS. Normal protein intake is essential. Students commonly write protein restriction.

steps.text: Prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day PO (maximum 60 mg/day regardless of weight) × 6 weeks. This is Phase 1 of the ISKD protocol.
steps.trap: Max cap = 60 mg/day. A 42 kg child would calculate 84 mg (2×42) but receives 60 mg. Always apply the cap.

steps.text: Assess response at week 4: remission = trace or nil proteinuria on dipstick. If no response by week 4–6 → steroid resistant NS → plan renal biopsy.
If responded: taper to Prednisolone 1.5 mg/kg on alternate days × 6 weeks (Phase 2). Total course: 12 weeks for full responders.
steps.trap: Phase 2 = 1.5 mg/kg alternate days. NOT 1 mg/kg. Total 12 weeks total — not 6 weeks only.

steps.text: Manage oedema: Furosemide for severe oedema. For hypovolaemia (cold peripheries, tachycardia): 25% albumin 0.5–1.0 g/kg IV over 1–2 h + loop diuretic after.
steps.trap: Albumin is for HYPOVOLAEMIA — not routine oedema. Giving albumin for routine oedema causes pulmonary oedema.

steps.text: Vaccinate before steroids: Pneumococcal vaccine + Varicella vaccine. For SBP/infection: Ceftriaxone. For thrombosis: LMWH then Warfarin. ACE inhibitors for persistent HTN/proteinuria.
steps.trap: S. pneumoniae is the #1 SBP organism in NS. Must vaccinate BEFORE starting steroids.

steps.text: Confirm severe dehydration: lethargic or unconscious, unable to drink, sunken eyes, skin pinch goes back very slowly (>2 sec). Admit immediately.
IV access immediately. If unable to establish IV → intraosseous access. Send electrolytes and BGA to rule out hypernatraemic dehydration.
PHASE 1 — Child 1–5 years: Ringer's Lactate 30 mL/kg IV over 30 minutes. Reassess after each phase.
steps.trap: Child (1–5 yr) = 30 mL/kg over 30 MIN. Infant (<12 months) = 30 mL/kg over 1 HOUR. AGE MATTERS — the most common swap error.

steps.text: Reassess after Phase 1: if improving → continue Phase 2. If not improving → repeat Phase 1 bolus. If in shock → give 10–20 mL/kg NS bolus first.
PHASE 2: Ringer's Lactate 70 mL/kg IV over 5 hours. Continue monitoring every 1–2 hours.
steps.trap: Total Plan C = 30 mL/kg (30 min) + 70 mL/kg (5 hours) = 100 mL/kg total. Know both phases and their durations.

steps.text: When child can drink: Start ORS 5 mL/kg/hour orally alongside remaining IV fluids. Transition to Plan B/A as condition improves.
Nutritional support + adjuncts: continue breastfeeding throughout. Zinc 20 mg/day (≥6 months) or 10 mg/day (<6 months) × 10–14 days. Reassess every 1–2 hours throughout.
steps.trap: Zinc dose is AGE-DEPENDENT: ≥6 months = 20 mg/day. <6 months = 10 mg/day. High-yield MCQ trap.

steps.text: General: bed rest during acute phase. Salt and fluid restriction to insensible losses + urine output. Strict daily I/O chart, weight, and BP monitoring. Hospital admission for severe cases.
Streptococcal eradication: Benzathine Penicillin G — 600,000 U IM if weight <27 kg; 1.2 million U IM if weight ≥27 kg. Single dose. Alternative: Penicillin V 250 mg PO BD × 10 days.
steps.trap: Penicillin is for STREP ERADICATION — NOT treatment of GN itself. The GN is immune-mediated, not from active infection.

steps.text: Hypertension: First-line = Nifedipine (calcium channel blocker) oral. Emergency/severe HTN = IV Labetalol or IV Sodium Nitroprusside. Do NOT give ACE inhibitors.
steps.trap: ACE inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in acute AGN HTN — they worsen hyperkalaemia and AKI. First-line = Nifedipine CCB. This is the single most tested trap.

steps.text: Fluid overload: Furosemide 1–2 mg/kg IV. Do NOT use furosemide for hypertension — it is only for fluid overload. These are different indications.
steps.trap: Furosemide = FLUID OVERLOAD only. Nifedipine = HYPERTENSION only. Students swap these — instant -1 mark.

steps.text: Hyperkalaemia management (stepped approach): Step 1 → Calcium gluconate 10% (cardioprotection). Step 2 → Insulin + Glucose (shift K⁺ intracellularly). Step 3 → Sodium bicarbonate. Step 4 → Dialysis if refractory.
AKI / severe azotaemia: Dialysis if refractory fluid overload + no response to furosemide, severe K⁺ >6.5, or uraemic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis).
Monitoring + prognosis: C3 complement should normalise by 6–8 weeks. If C3 remains low beyond 8–12 weeks → suspect MPGN, arrange biopsy. NO steroids or immunosuppressants in APSGN.
steps.trap: Steroids in APSGN = instant -2 marks. C3 persistent >12 weeks = NOT PSGN, reassess diagnosis.

steps.text: Confirm SRNS: No remission after 4 weeks daily prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day (max 60 mg) OR 6 weeks alternate-day. Do NOT confuse with steroid dependence (relapse on taper).
steps.trap: SRNS = no remission at 4 weeks daily. Steroid dependence = relapse during taper. Different entities.

steps.text: Renal biopsy: Mandatory in all children with SRNS. Establish histology — FSGS (most common ~35-50%), MPGN (~10%), IgM nephropathy, mesangial proliferative.
steps.trap: Biopsy is MANDATORY in SRNS. Cannot assume MCD. FSGS is the most common histology.

steps.text: Genetic testing: NPHS1, NPHS2 (podocin), WT1 sequencing. Essential if congenital onset, family history, or before transplant planning.
steps.trap: NPHS2 (podocin) = most common genetic cause. FSGS = most common histology. This pairing is examiner gold.

steps.text: Calcineurin inhibitor first-line: Tacrolimus 0.1–0.2 mg/kg/day (target trough 5–10 ng/mL) OR Cyclosporine 3–5 mg/kg/day. Monitor renal function and drug levels.
steps.trap: Tacrolimus trough 5–10 ng/mL in maintenance. Monitor for nephrotoxicity. Continue 12–24 months with slow taper.

steps.text: ACE inhibitor / ARB: Enalapril or Losartan for renal protection. Reduces proteinuria independent of blood pressure. Give EVEN if normotensive.
steps.trap: ACEi/ARBs provide renal protection BEYOND BP control. Always mention them in SRNS management even if normotensive.

steps.text: Assess response to CNI at 3–6 months. If responded → continue CNI 12–24 months with slow taper. If no response → consider Rituximab (anti-CD20) 375 mg/m² × 1–2 doses.
steps.trap: Rituximab is indicated for calcineurin-dependent or frequently relapsing SRNS. Not first-line monotherapy.

steps.text: Supportive care: Salt restriction, loop diuretics (furosemide) for edema, IV albumin only for severe hypoalbuminaemia with circulatory compromise, pneumococcal prophylaxis, statins if persistent hyperlipidaemia.
steps.trap: IV albumin is ONLY for severe hypoalbuminaemia with circulatory compromise. Not for routine edema — risk of pulmonary edema.

steps.text: If progressive to ESRD: Transplant evaluation. Genetic SRNS (NPHS1/NPHS2) does NOT recur post-transplant. Sporadic FSGS has 30–50% recurrence risk — consider pre-emptive plasmapheresis.
steps.trap: Genetic SRNS does NOT recur after transplant — key distinction from sporadic FSGS (30–50% recurrence).

steps.text: Assess for SAM: Weight-for-height <–3 SD OR MUAC <11.5 cm OR bilateral pitting edema (any grade). Check for danger signs.
steps.trap: SAM criteria: WFH <–3 SD OR MUAC <11.5 cm OR edema. Any ONE criterion confirms SAM.

steps.text: Check danger signs on admission: Hypoglycaemia, hypothermia, dehydration, shock, severe infection. Check blood glucose immediately.
steps.trap: Hypoglycaemia is the most dangerous complication. Always check and treat immediately on admission.

steps.text: Treat hypoglycaemia: Give 5 mL/kg of 10% glucose ORS orally OR 2 mL/kg IV dextrose if unconscious. Then feed every 2 hours.
steps.trap: Feed every 2 hours after hypoglycaemia treatment. Do NOT leave the child fasting.

steps.text: Treat dehydration: Use ReSoMal (reduced sodium ORS) — 5 mL/kg every 30 min for 2h, then 5–10 mL/kg/hr. Do NOT use standard ORS.
steps.trap: Standard ORS has too much sodium for SAM. Use ReSoMal (reduced sodium ORS) or dilute standard ORS.

steps.text: Treat sepsis: Broad-spectrum antibiotics for ALL SAM patients — Ampicillin + Gentamicin first-line. Avoid ceftriaxone in infants <1 month.
steps.trap: Give antibiotics to ALL SAM patients even without obvious infection — immune dysfunction masks signs.

steps.text: Phase 1 feeds: F-75 therapeutic milk 130 mL/kg/day divided every 2 hours. F-75 = 75 kcal/100 mL, 0.9g protein/100 mL. Do NOT start iron yet.
steps.trap: Phase 1 = F-75 LOW calorie (130 mL/kg/day). Do NOT give high-calorie feeds yet — risk of refeeding syndrome. NO iron in Phase 1.

steps.text: Phase 2 feeds (when stabilized): F-100 therapeutic milk or RUTF 150–220 kcal/kg/day. Start iron ONLY after edema resolves. Add micronutrients.
steps.trap: Start iron ONLY after edema resolves (Phase 2). Iron in Phase 1 increases oxidative stress and infection risk.

steps.text: Discharge criteria: Weight-for-height >–2 SD, eating well, no edema for 2 weeks, caregiver educated. Continue RUTF (Plumpy'Nut) at home with weekly follow-up.
steps.trap: Discharge only when WFH >–2 SD AND no edema for 2 weeks. Premature discharge = relapse.

steps.text: Grade HE (West Haven): Grade 0 = subclinical; Grade 1 = mild confusion; Grade 2 = lethargy + asterixis; Grade 3 = stupor; Grade 4 = coma. Intubate for Grade 3–4.
steps.trap: Grade 0 (minimal HE) is diagnosed only by psychometric testing — not absence of disease.

steps.text: Identify and treat precipitant using HEPATICS mnemonic: Haemorrhage (GI bleed), Electrolytes (hypokalaemia), Protein load, Ascites infection (SBP), Toxins (sedatives), Infection, Constipation, Surgery.
steps.trap: Treating the precipitant is the cornerstone of HE management. Do NOT just give lactulose without removing the trigger.

steps.text: Lactulose 0.5–1 mL/kg/dose, 2–3 times daily. Titrate to 2–3 soft stools/day. Mechanism: acidifies colonic pH → converts NH₃ to NH₄⁺ (non-absorbable).
steps.trap: Titrate lactulose to 2–3 soft stools/day. Too little = no ammonia reduction. Too much = dehydration.

steps.text: Rifaximin 10–20 mg/kg/day. Non-absorbable antibiotic reducing ammonia-producing gut flora. Synergistic with lactulose — superior to lactulose alone.
steps.trap: Combination of lactulose + rifaximin is SUPERIOR to lactulose alone. Always mention both in management.

steps.text: Airway protection: Endotracheal intubation for Grade 3–4 HE. Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV for cerebral oedema. Elevate head 30°. Reduce fluid rate.
steps.trap: Both mannitol AND 3% saline are valid for cerebral oedema. Must also reduce fluid rate simultaneously.

steps.text: Protein: Do NOT routinely restrict protein. Maintain 1–1.5 g/kg/day normally. Only restrict during acute severe episodes. Long-term restriction causes sarcopenia and worsens ammonia.
steps.trap: Never recommend long-term protein restriction in chronic HE. It causes malnutrition and sarcopenia, paradoxically worsening ammonia levels.

steps.text: Refractory/recurrent HE: Evaluate for liver transplantation. Recurrent HE despite optimal medical therapy indicates advanced liver decompensation.
steps.trap: Refractory HE = indication for expedited liver transplant listing. Do not delay evaluation.

name: Hyponatremia Emergency
sub: 7 steps · volume status first · slow correction

steps.text: Confirm true hyponatraemia: Serum Na⁺ <135 mEq/L. Check serum osmolality to rule out pseudohyponatraemia (high lipids/proteins) or hyperglycaemia.
steps.trap: Hyperglycaemia causes pseudohyponatraemia — correct measured Na⁺ by adding 1.6 mEq/L for each 100 mg/dL glucose above 100.

steps.text: Assess volume status: Hypovolaemic (dry mucosa, tachycardia) vs Euvolaemic (normal skin turgor) vs Hypervolaemic (oedema, ascites). This determines the entire management pathway.
steps.trap: Volume status is the examiner's preferred framework. Getting this wrong leads to completely wrong management.

steps.text: Hypovolaemic: Replace volume with normal saline. Once euvolaemic, free water excretion resumes and Na⁺ corrects. For CSW: aggressive NaCl + fludrocortisone.
steps.trap: CSW is hypovolaemic — fluid restriction causes SHOCK. Replace Na⁺ + volume aggressively.

steps.text: Euvolaemic (SIADH): Fluid restriction 800–1200 mL/m²/day (60–75% maintenance). First-line for mild-moderate SIADH. Normal saline may WORSEN hyponatraemia.
steps.trap: Normal saline is hypotonic relative to urine in SIADH (urine osmolality >300) — may worsen hyponatraemia. Use fluid restriction or hypertonic saline.

steps.text: Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures, Na⁺ <120): 3% hypertonic saline 1–2 mL/kg/hr. Calculate rate: [desired rise/hr × 0.6 × weight] / 0.513.
steps.trap: Check Na⁺ every 2–4 hours during 3% saline infusion. Rapid correction risks osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS).

steps.text: Rate of correction: Chronic hyponatraemia (>48h) — max 8–10 mEq/L in 24h and 12–18 mEq/L in 48h. Acute symptomatic: can correct faster initially (1–2 mEq/L/hr for 3–4h).
steps.trap: Rapid correction >12 mEq/L/24h causes central pontine myelinolysis (locked-in syndrome, quadriplegia). Max = 8–10 mEq/L/24h for chronic.

steps.text: Treat underlying cause: SIADH → stop offending drugs, treat CNS infection/pneumonia. CSW → treat CNS trauma/SAH. Hypervolaemic → treat CHF/cirrhosis/NS.
steps.trap: Always treat the underlying cause. Hyponatraemia will recur if the precipitant is not addressed.

steps.text: Assess toxicity: Single ingestion >150 mg/kg OR chronic >75 mg/kg/day. Risk factors: fasting, malnutrition, enzyme-inducing drugs (phenytoin, rifampicin).
steps.trap: Chronic supratherapeutic dosing (>75 mg/kg/day) can cause toxicity — especially in malnourished or fasting children.

steps.text: Gastric decontamination: Activated charcoal 1 g/kg (max 50 g) if within 1–2 hours of ingestion AND airway is protected.
steps.trap: Activated charcoal reduces absorption by 50–90% but only if given within 1–2 hours. Do NOT delay NAC for charcoal if >8 hours since ingestion.

steps.text: NAC indication: All potentially toxic ingestions OR serum level above treatment line on Rumack-Matthew nomogram (4–24h post-ingestion) OR abnormal LFTs/INR.
steps.trap: Rumack-Matthew nomogram applies ONLY to single acute ingestions between 4–24 hours. Cannot use for chronic or staggered overdoses.

steps.text: NAC IV regimen (preferred in children): 150 mg/kg in 3 mL/kg D5W over 1h → 50 mg/kg in 7 mL/kg D5W over 4h → 100 mg/kg in 14 mL/kg D5W over 16h. Total 300 mg/kg/21h.
steps.trap: IV total = 300 mg/kg (150+50+100). Oral total = 1330 mg/kg. Do NOT confuse the regimens. Monitor for fluid overload in small children.

steps.text: NAC timing: MOST effective within 8 hours of ingestion. Still beneficial beyond 8h if hepatic failure has not developed. Give NAC empirically if time of ingestion is uncertain.
steps.trap: Do NOT delay NAC for lab results or charcoal if >8 hours since ingestion. The 8-hour window is critical for efficacy.

steps.text: Monitor LFTs, INR, creatinine, glucose every 4–6 hours. INR >3 at 48h or >4.5 at any time = poor prognosis. pH <7.3 after fluid resuscitation = poor prognosis.
steps.trap: INR is a prognostic marker. FFP only for active bleeding — NOT for INR correction alone (masks prognostic value).

steps.text: King's College poor prognostic criteria: (1) Arterial pH <7.3 after fluids OR (2) All three of: INR >6.5, creatinine >3.4 mg/dL, encephalopathy Grade III–IV.
steps.trap: King's College criteria indicate need for liver transplant evaluation. Do not delay referral if criteria are met.

steps.text: Liver transplant: For fulminant hepatic failure meeting King's College criteria. Contact transplant centre early. Complete hepatic regeneration is usual if patient survives.
steps.trap: Paracetamol poisoning has excellent prognosis if NAC given within 8h. Late presentation (>24h) has 5–10% mortality without transplant.

steps.text: Recognise crisis: Virilised female with vomiting, dehydration, shock at 7–14 days of life. Hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + metabolic acidosis. THIS IS A MEDICAL EMERGENCY.
steps.trap: Salt-wasting crisis at 7–14 days is a neonatal emergency. Do NOT delay treatment for confirmatory tests.

steps.text: Emergency resuscitation: 0.9% NS 10–20 mL/kg boluses for hypovolaemic shock. IV access. Send bloods: glucose, electrolytes, cortisol, 17-OHP.
steps.trap: Do NOT give potassium-containing fluids. The child already has hyperkalaemia. Use normal saline for resuscitation.

steps.text: Hydrocortisone stress dose: 50–100 mg/m²/day IV divided Q6H for 24–48 hours. This is 5–10× the maintenance dose. Then taper to maintenance.
steps.trap: Stress dose = 50–100 mg/m²/day IV. Maintenance = 10–15 mg/m²/day PO. These are vastly different — do NOT confuse them.

steps.text: Fludrocortisone: 0.1–0.2 mg/day PO when enteral tolerated. Mineralocorticoid replacement for aldosterone deficiency.
steps.trap: Fludrocortisone is ONLY needed in salt-wasting CAH. Simple virilising CAH may not need it. Monitor BP and electrolytes.

steps.text: Sodium chloride: 1–2 g/day PO (or IV saline if shocked). Infants have higher sodium requirement due to immature kidneys. May discontinue in older children.
steps.trap: Infants need 1–2 g/day NaCl. Older children may not need supplemental salt if diet is adequate.

steps.text: Hyperkalaemia management: Calcium gluconate for cardioprotection → insulin + glucose → sodium bicarbonate if pH <7.1. Dialysis for refractory cases.
steps.trap: Hyperkalaemia in CAH is due to aldosterone deficiency. Fludrocortisone is the definitive treatment — adjuncts are temporary bridges.
